questions.question: Which sub-clauses of Section 536(2) preserve pending proceedings under the old Act?

options.text: Section 536(2)(a) and (b)
Section 536(2)(c) and (e)
Section 536(2)(d) and (f)
Section 536(2)(k) and (m)

questions.explanation: Section 536(2)(c) preserves pending assessments, reassessments, appeals, and penalties for pre-April 2026 years. Section 536(2)(e) ensures proceedings pending before any authority, Tribunal, or Court continue as if the new Act had not been enacted. Together, they form the core savings rule for pending proceedings.
questions.explanationWrong: The correct answer is Section 536(2)(c) and (e). Sub-clause (c) covers pending proceedings for pre-April 2026 years, and sub-clause (e) covers proceedings before any authority or court. Sub-clause (d) is specifically for penalties, (k) is about expired limitation, and (m)/(n) deal with loss carry-forward.

questions.question: An assessment order is passed in 2028 for AY 2024-25. Which Act governs the assessment?

options.text: ITA 2025 because the order is passed after April 1, 2026
ITA 1961 because it is for a pre-April 2026 assessment year
Both Acts apply simultaneously
ITA 2025 because AY 2024-25 maps to Tax Year 2023-24

questions.explanation: Section 536(2)(c) is clear: proceedings for pre-April 2026 years continue under the old Act regardless of when the order is passed. The date of the order is irrelevant — what matters is the assessment year in question.
questions.explanationWrong: The governing Act is determined by the assessment year, not the date the order is passed. AY 2024-25 is a pre-April 2026 year, so the old Act applies even if the order comes years later.

questions.question: In Unit 2.1, we learned about the dual-track system. Which section of the 2025 Act formally repeals the Income Tax Act, 1961?

options.text: Section 534
Section 535
Section 536(1)
Section 537

questions.explanation: Section 536(1) states: 'The Income-tax Act, 1961 is hereby repealed.' The remaining sub-sections of 536 then carve out extensive savings to ensure the old Act's provisions survive for pre-April 2026 years.
questions.explanationWrong: Section 536(1) is the repeal provision. The savings clauses that preserve old Act proceedings are in Section 536(2) onwards.

questions.question: Under Section 536(2)(k), what happens to limitation periods that have already expired under the old Act before April 1, 2026?

options.text: They are extended by one year under the new Act
They are revived with new time limits under the 2025 Act
They are NOT revived — expired limitation stays expired
They are revived only for reassessment proceedings

questions.explanation: Section 536(2)(k) explicitly provides that expired limitation is not revived by the new Act. If the time for filing an appeal or initiating reassessment has already run out under the old Act, the new Act does not grant a second chance.
questions.explanationWrong: The principle is clear: expired limitation stays expired. Section 536(2)(k) prevents the new Act from being used to revive time-barred proceedings, whether for reassessment, appeals, or any other purpose.

questions.question: Which types of proceedings are preserved under the old Act by Section 536(2)(c) and (e)?

options.text: Only assessments and reassessments
Only appeals and revisions
Assessments, reassessments, appeals, penalties, revisions, and references
Only proceedings that were pending on April 1, 2026

questions.explanation: The scope is comprehensive: assessments, reassessments, rectification, penalty, revision, reference, and appeals are all covered. Importantly, this applies both to proceedings pending on April 1, 2026 AND to proceedings initiated after that date for pre-April 2026 years.
questions.explanationWrong: The savings clause is broad — it covers every type of income tax proceeding, not just some. Also note that it applies to both already-pending proceedings and freshly-initiated proceedings for old AYs.

questions.question: Old Section 147 (power to assess/reassess escaped income) maps to which section in the 2025 Act?

options.text: Section 277
Section 279
Section 281
Section 286

questions.explanation: Section 147 maps to Section 279 in the 2025 Act. The reassessment sections map sequentially: 147→279, 148→280, 148A→281, 149→282, 150→283, 151→284, 152→285, 153→286.
questions.explanationWrong: The correct mapping is 147→279. Remember the pattern: the reassessment block starts at 279 in the new Act and runs through 286, corresponding to old Sections 147-153.

questions.question: The show-cause procedure before issuing a reassessment notice was in old Section 148A. What is its new section number?

options.text: Section 280
Section 281
Section 282
Section 284

questions.explanation: Section 148A maps to Section 281 in the 2025 Act. The show-cause procedure — where the AO must provide information and give the assessee an opportunity to respond before issuing a reassessment notice — is structurally the same.
questions.explanationWrong: Old Section 148A maps to new Section 281. Section 280 is the notice for reassessment (old 148), and Section 282 is time limits (old 149).

questions.question: In Unit 2.1, we learned that Section 536(3) bridges terminology for lookback calculations. What does 'previous year' under the old Act become under the new Act?

options.text: Assessment Year
Financial Year
Tax Year
Income Year

questions.explanation: Section 536(3) ensures that references to prior 'tax years' in the 2025 Act can look back to 'previous years' under the old Act. Without this bridge, multi-year lookback tests would have no historical data to reference.
questions.explanationWrong: 'Previous year' becomes 'tax year' under the 2025 Act. This is the standard terminology change. Section 536(3) ensures this bridge works for lookback calculations.

questions.question: Old Section 151 (sanction for issue of reassessment notice) maps to which new section?

options.text: Section 282
Section 283
Section 284
Section 285

questions.explanation: Section 151 maps to Section 284. The requirement for prior approval from a specified authority before issuing a reassessment notice is retained in the new Act.
questions.explanationWrong: The mapping is 151→284. Remember: 149→282 (time limits), 150→283 (appellate/court orders), 151→284 (sanction), 152→285 (rate of tax/dropping).

questions.question: How many old sections are mapped in the reassessment block (147-153 → 279-286)?

options.text: 6 sections
7 sections
8 sections
9 sections

questions.explanation: Eight sections: 147→279, 148→280, 148A→281, 149→282, 150→283, 151→284, 152→285, 153→286. Note that 148A counts as a separate section, so the old block is 147, 148, 148A, 149, 150, 151, 152, 153 — eight in total.
questions.explanationWrong: Count carefully: 147, 148, 148A, 149, 150, 151, 152, 153 — that's eight sections. Don't forget Section 148A, which is a distinct section introduced by Finance Act 2021.

questions.question: Old Section 246 (appeal to JCIT(A)/CIT(A)) maps to which section in the 2025 Act?

options.text: Section 354
Section 356
Section 361
Section 365

questions.explanation: Section 246 (and 246A) maps to Section 356 (and 356(3)) in the 2025 Act. The first-level appeal to JCIT(A) and CIT(A) continues in the same framework.
questions.explanationWrong: The mapping is 246→356. Section 361 is the ITAT appeal (old 253), and Section 365 is the High Court appeal (old 260A).

questions.question: The appeal to the Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) was governed by old Section 253. What is its new section number?

options.text: Section 358
Section 359
Section 361
Section 363

questions.explanation: Section 253 maps to Section 361 in the 2025 Act. Appeals to ITAT continue under the same framework with no substantive change to the appellate hierarchy.
questions.explanationWrong: The correct mapping is 253→361. The appeals mapping follows a sequence: 246→356 (first appeal), 253→361 (ITAT), 260A→365 (HC), 261→368 (SC).

questions.question: In Unit 2.1, we discussed that the e-filing portal will support both Acts concurrently. What is this arrangement commonly called?

options.text: The single-track system
The dual-track system
The sunset system
The migration system

questions.explanation: The dual-track system means both ITA 1961 and ITA 2025 operate simultaneously. The portal supports filings under both Acts — old Act for pre-April 2026 years, new Act for Tax Year 2026-27 onwards.
questions.explanationWrong: It's called the dual-track system. Two independent compliance tracks operate in parallel — old Act for pre-2026 years, new Act for 2026-27 onwards.

questions.question: Old Section 260A (appeal to High Court) maps to which new section?

options.text: Section 363
Section 365
Section 368
Section 370

questions.explanation: Section 260A maps to Section 365. The High Court appeal provisions continue with no substantive change. The appellate hierarchy — AO → JCIT(A)/CIT(A) → ITAT → HC → SC — remains identical.
questions.explanationWrong: The correct mapping is 260A→365. Section 368 is the Supreme Court appeal (old 261). The hierarchy is unchanged.

questions.question: Has the appellate hierarchy changed under the 2025 Act?

options.text: Yes, a new National Appellate Authority has been added
Yes, ITAT has been replaced by a Tax Tribunal
No, the hierarchy AO → JCIT(A)/CIT(A) → ITAT → HC → SC is unchanged
Yes, High Court appeals have been abolished

questions.explanation: The appellate hierarchy is identical: AO → JCIT(A)/CIT(A) → ITAT → High Court → Supreme Court. Only the section numbers have changed; the structure and sequence of appeals remain the same.
questions.explanationWrong: No structural change — the five-level hierarchy is exactly the same. The section numbers changed (246→356, 253→361, 260A→365, 261→368) but the institutions and sequence are unchanged.

lessons.title: Reassessment Time Limits — Old vs New

questions.question: Under the old Act, what was the time limit for issuing a show-cause notice for reassessment where escaped income is below the monetary threshold?

options.text: 2 years from end of AY
3 years from end of AY
4 years from end of AY
5 years from end of AY

questions.explanation: Under the old Act (Section 149), the show-cause notice for escaped income below the monetary threshold had to be issued within 3 years from the end of the relevant assessment year.
questions.explanationWrong: The old Act limit was 3 years from the end of AY for below-threshold cases. The new Act extends this to 4 years from the end of the tax year.

questions.question: How have the reassessment time limits changed from old Act to new Act for cases at or above the monetary threshold?

options.text: Show-cause: 5→6 years; Notice: 5y3m→6y3m
Show-cause: 5→7 years; Notice: 5y3m→7y3m
Show-cause: 3→5 years; Notice: 3y3m→5y3m
No change — same time limits under both Acts

questions.explanation: For above-threshold cases, the show-cause notice window goes from 5 years (old) to 6 years (new), and the reassessment notice from 5 years 3 months (old) to 6 years 3 months (new). The extension is exactly one year in each case.
questions.explanationWrong: The pattern is consistent: each time limit is extended by exactly one year. Show-cause goes from 5 to 6 years, and the reassessment notice from 5y3m to 6y3m.

questions.question: In Unit 2.1, we learned that old circulars and notifications continue under Section 536(2)(j). What is the condition for their continued validity?

options.text: They must be re-issued by CBDT within 6 months
They must not be inconsistent with the new Act
They are valid only for one year after April 1, 2026
They must be specifically listed in a Schedule

questions.explanation: Section 536(2)(j) preserves old circulars, notifications, and approvals provided they are not inconsistent with the new Act. No re-issuance is required — they continue automatically.
questions.explanationWrong: The condition is simple: they continue unless inconsistent with the new Act. There's no re-issuance requirement, no time limit, and no need to be listed in a Schedule.

questions.question: Under the new Act, what is the reassessment notice time limit for escaped income below ₹50 lakh?

options.text: 3 years 3 months from end of Tax Year
4 years from end of Tax Year
4 years 3 months from end of Tax Year
5 years 3 months from end of Tax Year

questions.explanation: Under Section 280 read with Section 282, the reassessment notice for below-₹50-lakh cases must be issued within 4 years 3 months from the end of the tax year. The old limit was 3 years 3 months from the end of AY.
questions.explanationWrong: It's 4 years 3 months — one year more than the old limit of 3 years 3 months. Don't confuse the show-cause limit (4 years) with the reassessment notice limit (4 years 3 months).

questions.question: By how many years has each reassessment time limit been extended in the new Act compared to the old Act?

options.text: 6 months
1 year
2 years
3 months

questions.explanation: Every time limit has been extended by exactly one year: show-cause goes from 3→4 and 5→6 years, reassessment notice from 3y3m→4y3m and 5y3m→6y3m. The 3-month gap between show-cause and reassessment notice remains constant.
questions.explanationWrong: The extension is uniformly one year across all categories. The old 3/5-year structure becomes 4/6 years, and the 3-month extra for reassessment notice is unchanged.

questions.question: What new safeguard does Section 282(3) of the 2025 Act introduce for reassessment?

options.text: Mandatory approval from PCIT before any reassessment
No reassessment notice within 1 year from end of the tax year
Assessee must be given 90 days to respond to show-cause notice
Reassessment can only be initiated after a full audit

questions.explanation: Section 282(3) introduces a one-year moratorium: no reassessment notice can be issued within one year from the end of any tax year. This prevents reassessment from being initiated before the original return due date has passed — a safeguard not present in the old Act.
questions.explanationWrong: Section 282(3) is a timing safeguard — it creates a one-year cooling-off period. The AO cannot issue a reassessment notice until at least one year after the tax year ends.

questions.question: What is the minimum residual period rule introduced by Section 377(7) for Commissioner's revision?

options.text: 30 days remaining after revision order for filing appeal
45 days remaining after revision order for filing appeal
60 days remaining after revision order for filing appeal
90 days remaining after revision order for filing appeal

questions.explanation: Section 377(7) requires that after the Commissioner passes a revision order, the assessee must have at least 60 days remaining in the limitation period to file any consequent appeal or application. This prevents assessees from being time-barred by a late revision order.
questions.explanationWrong: The minimum is 60 days. This is a new procedural right — old Section 263 had no such safeguard, and assessees could be left with insufficient time to appeal after a revision.

questions.question: In Unit 2.1, Section 536(2)(f) was discussed regarding elections and declarations. What happens to options exercised under the old Act?

options.text: They become void and must be re-exercised
They are deemed exercised under the corresponding new Act provision
They continue only for one transitional year
They must be confirmed by CBDT notification

questions.explanation: Section 536(2)(f) provides that elections, declarations, and options validly exercised under the old Act are deemed to have been exercised under the corresponding provision of the new Act. No re-exercise is required.
questions.explanationWrong: Elections carry over automatically — they are deemed exercised under the corresponding new provision. There's no need to re-exercise, no time limit, and no CBDT confirmation.

questions.question: Why does Section 282(3) prevent reassessment within one year from the end of a tax year?

options.text: To allow time for TDS/TCS credit matching
To ensure the original return due date has passed before reassessment can begin
To allow the AO to complete regular assessment first
To reduce the department's workload in the first year

questions.explanation: The one-year moratorium ensures that reassessment is not initiated before the assessee has even had a chance to file the original return. Since the return due date falls within the first year after the tax year ends, Section 282(3) prevents premature action by the AO.
questions.explanationWrong: The purpose is to prevent premature reassessment. The assessee should at least have had the opportunity to file the original return before the AO can initiate reassessment proceedings.

questions.question: Section 377(7)'s 60-day residual period rule applies when which authority passes a revision order?

options.text: The Assessing Officer
The ITAT
The Commissioner (PCIT/CIT)
The High Court

questions.explanation: Section 377 deals with revision by the Commissioner (PCIT/CIT), corresponding to old Section 263. The 60-day rule ensures the assessee is not left without time to appeal against a revision order passed close to the limitation deadline.
questions.explanationWrong: Revision is the Commissioner's power (old Section 263, new Section 377). The AO doesn't revise — the AO rectifies. ITAT and High Court hear appeals, they don't exercise revision power.

questions.question: A Section 148A(1) show-cause notice was issued on March 15, 2026. Can the AO issue the Section 148 reassessment notice after April 1, 2026?

options.text: No — the new Act applies after April 1, 2026
Yes — under the old Act, as the proceeding was already initiated
Yes — but under the new Section 280
No — the show-cause must be restarted under Section 281

questions.explanation: Section 536(2)(c) preserves in-progress proceedings. A show-cause notice under old Section 148A(1) issued before April 1, 2026 is a pending proceeding — the AO can continue and issue the Section 148 notice after April 1, 2026 under the old Act.
questions.explanationWrong: The proceeding was initiated under the old Act before the cutoff. Section 536(2)(c) allows it to continue under the old Act — no restart under the new Act is needed or required.

questions.question: After April 1, 2026, the department wants to initiate fresh reassessment for AY 2023-24 (no prior proceedings exist). Is this possible?

options.text: No — fresh proceedings for old AYs cannot begin after April 1, 2026
Yes — under the new Act's Sections 279-286
Yes — under the old Act, provided limitation under the old Act has not expired
Only with special CBDT approval

questions.explanation: Fresh reassessment for pre-April 2026 AYs can be initiated even after April 1, 2026, but under the old Act's provisions (Sections 147-153). The critical check is whether the limitation period under the old Act has expired — if it has, Section 536(2)(k) prevents revival.
questions.explanationWrong: The old Act governs all proceedings for pre-April 2026 years, whether pending or freshly initiated. The only bar is if the limitation has already expired under the old Act.

questions.question: In Unit 2.1, what did we learn happens when old Act deduction/exemption conditions are violated after April 1, 2026 under Section 536(2)(h)?

options.text: The violation is ignored since the old Act is repealed
The amount is deemed income under the new Act in the year of violation
The old deduction is recalculated under old Act rates
A penalty is imposed under the old Act

questions.explanation: Section 536(2)(h) provides that if conditions for old Act deductions/exemptions are violated after April 2026, the relevant amount is deemed income under the 2025 Act in the year of violation. This prevents condition violations from falling into a legal vacuum.
questions.explanationWrong: The violation triggers income recognition under the new Act. The amount becomes taxable income in the year of violation — neither ignored nor handled under the old Act.

questions.question: ITAT remands a case for AY 2022-23 back to the AO in July 2027. Under which Act does the AO conduct the remand proceedings?

options.text: ITA 2025 since the proceedings are after April 1, 2026
ITA 1961 since it is for a pre-April 2026 assessment year
ITA 2025 but applying old Act rates
Whichever Act the ITAT specifies in its order

questions.explanation: Remand proceedings for old AYs are governed by the old Act. The assessment year determines the governing Act, not when the remand order is passed or when the AO re-does the assessment. ITAT doesn't get to choose — the law is clear.
questions.explanationWrong: The governing Act depends on the assessment year, not the date of the proceedings. AY 2022-23 is a pre-April 2026 year, so the old Act applies to all proceedings including remands.

questions.question: For Tax Year 2026-27, reassessment proceedings will be governed by which set of sections?

options.text: Old Sections 147-153
New Sections 279-286
Both old and new sections concurrently
New Sections 356-378

questions.explanation: Tax Year 2026-27 is the first year fully under the 2025 Act. Reassessment is governed by Sections 279-286 of the new Act. Sections 356-378 are the appeals provisions, not reassessment.
questions.explanationWrong: Sections 279-286 govern reassessment under the new Act. The new Act applies to Tax Year 2026-27 onwards. Don't confuse with Sections 356-378, which are for appeals.

questions.question: An AO discovers escaped income of ₹60 lakh for AY 2024-25 in October 2027. Under the old Act, what is the maximum time for issuing a show-cause notice under Section 148A(1)?

options.text: March 31, 2028 (3 years from end of AY)
March 31, 2030 (5 years from end of AY)
March 31, 2031 (6 years from end of AY)
March 31, 2029 (4 years from end of AY)

questions.explanation: AY 2024-25 ends on March 31, 2025. ₹60 lakh is at or above the monetary threshold, so the old Act's 5-year limit applies: show-cause notice must be issued by March 31, 2030 (5 years from March 31, 2025). The new Act's 6-year limit does not apply — old Act governs pre-April 2026 AYs.
questions.explanationWrong: Since AY 2024-25 is pre-April 2026, the old Act governs. ₹60 lakh is above threshold → 5-year limit → deadline is March 31, 2030. Don't apply new Act limits to old AYs.

questions.question: For Tax Year 2027-28, an AO wants to issue a reassessment notice for escaped income of ₹30 lakh. What is the earliest date the notice can be issued under Section 282(3)?

options.text: April 1, 2028 — start of the next tax year
April 1, 2029 — one year from end of tax year
July 31, 2028 — after the return due date
March 31, 2029 — end of the next tax year

questions.explanation: Tax Year 2027-28 ends March 31, 2028. Section 282(3) prohibits reassessment notice within one year from the end of the tax year. So the earliest date is April 1, 2029. This is a new safeguard not present in the old Act.
questions.explanationWrong: Section 282(3)'s one-year moratorium means no notice until at least one year after March 31, 2028 — that's April 1, 2029. The return due date is irrelevant for this calculation.

questions.question: In Unit 2.1, we learned about Section 536(2)(v). If a search was initiated under old Section 132 before April 1, 2026, under which Act are the consequent assessment proceedings conducted?

options.text: ITA 2025 since search assessments are special proceedings
ITA 1961 — the entire search proceeding continues under old Act
ITA 2025 with old Act rates applied
Whichever Act gives the department a longer limitation

questions.explanation: Section 536(2)(v) specifically preserves search proceedings under old Section 132. The entire chain — search, seizure, assessment, penalty — continues under the old Act if the search was initiated before April 1, 2026.
questions.explanationWrong: Section 536(2)(v) is explicit: search proceedings initiated under old Section 132 continue entirely under the old Act. The department doesn't get to choose the more favorable Act.

questions.question: An AO issues a show-cause notice on March 20, 2026 under old Section 148A(1) for AY 2021-22, but the assessee's response comes on April 15, 2026. The AO then issues the Section 148 notice on May 1, 2026. Is the reassessment notice valid?

options.text: No — the Section 148 notice was issued after April 1, 2026, so new Act applies
No — the AO should have issued the notice before April 1, 2026
Yes — the proceeding was pending on April 1, 2026, so old Act continues to apply
Yes — but only if the AO obtains CBDT approval

questions.explanation: The show-cause under Section 148A(1) was issued before April 1, 2026, making it a pending proceeding. Section 536(2)(c) allows the AO to continue under the old Act — the Section 148 notice issued on May 1, 2026 is valid under the old Act.
questions.explanationWrong: The proceeding was already initiated (show-cause issued March 20, 2026). It was pending on April 1, 2026. Section 536(2)(c) preserves it — the AO can issue Section 148 notice after April 1, 2026 under the old Act.

questions.question: For AY 2020-21, the old Act's 3-year limitation for show-cause notice expired on March 31, 2024. On April 15, 2026, the AO wants to initiate reassessment for escaped income of ₹20 lakh. Can the AO proceed?

options.text: Yes — the new Act provides a longer 4-year window
Yes — Section 536(2)(c) allows fresh proceedings for old AYs
No — Section 536(2)(k) prevents revival of expired limitation
Yes — if PCIT grants special approval

questions.explanation: The 3-year limitation expired on March 31, 2024 (3 years from end of AY 2020-21, i.e., March 31, 2021). Section 536(2)(k) is clear: expired limitation is NOT revived by the new Act. The new Act's 4-year limit does not help because it applies only to tax years starting April 1, 2026.
questions.explanationWrong: Once limitation expires under the old Act, it's gone forever. Section 536(2)(k) explicitly prevents the new Act from reviving expired time limits. The new Act's longer windows apply only to new tax years.

questions.question: An appeal against an assessment order for AY 2025-26 is filed with CIT(A) on June 1, 2026. Which Act governs this appeal?

options.text: ITA 2025 because the appeal is filed after April 1, 2026
ITA 1961 because AY 2025-26 is a pre-April 2026 year
ITA 2025 with old Section 246A procedures
The assessee gets to choose which Act

questions.explanation: AY 2025-26 is the last assessment year under the old Act. Even though the appeal is filed after April 1, 2026, the governing Act is determined by the assessment year — old Act applies. The appeal is filed under old Section 246/246A, not new Section 356.
questions.explanationWrong: The assessment year determines the governing Act. AY 2025-26 is pre-April 2026, so old Act governs regardless of when the appeal is filed. The assessee has no choice in the matter.

questions.question: The Commissioner passes a revision order under Section 377 on February 15, 2029 for Tax Year 2026-27. The limitation for filing an appeal expires on March 31, 2029. Does Section 377(7) protect the assessee?

options.text: No — the assessee has 44 days, which is sufficient
Yes — the assessee has only 44 days, less than the required 60 days
No — Section 377(7) only applies to old Act cases
Yes — but the assessee must apply to extend limitation

questions.explanation: From February 15 to March 31, 2029 is only 44 days — less than the 60-day minimum required by Section 377(7). The provision automatically ensures the assessee gets at least 60 days, effectively extending the deadline to April 16, 2029.
questions.explanationWrong: 44 days is less than 60 days. Section 377(7) kicks in automatically — no application needed. The assessee gets at least 60 days from the date of the revision order to file an appeal.

questions.question: In Unit 2.1, we learned about the General Clauses Act safety net. Which section of the General Clauses Act applies to the repeal of the old Income Tax Act?

options.text: Section 4
Section 5
Section 6
Section 8

questions.explanation: Section 536(4) of the 2025 Act invokes Section 6 of the General Clauses Act, 1897 as a safety net. This covers any unforeseen situation not specifically addressed by Section 536(2)'s detailed savings provisions.
questions.explanationWrong: Section 6 of the General Clauses Act, 1897 deals with the effect of repeal of an enactment. Section 536(4) explicitly invokes it as a catch-all safety net.

questions.question: An ITAT appeal for AY 2023-24 is pending on April 1, 2026. ITAT disposes it on December 10, 2026. The taxpayer wants to file a further appeal to the High Court. Under which section does the HC appeal lie?

options.text: New Section 365 because the HC appeal is filed after April 1, 2026
Old Section 260A because the underlying AY is pre-April 2026
New Section 365 but with old Act procedures
The taxpayer must file under both sections

questions.explanation: The assessment year (AY 2023-24) determines the governing Act throughout the entire appellate chain. Even though the HC appeal will be filed well after April 1, 2026, old Section 260A governs because the underlying year is pre-April 2026.
questions.explanationWrong: The governing Act follows the assessment year through every level of appeal. AY 2023-24 = old Act throughout — whether at AO, CIT(A), ITAT, HC, or SC level.

questions.question: A penalty order for AY 2024-25 is passed on September 1, 2027. The taxpayer's appeal limitation under old Section 246 expires on October 31, 2027. The taxpayer files the appeal on October 25, 2027. Is the appeal validly filed?

options.text: No — the new Act's Section 356 should have been used
Yes — old Section 246 applies because AY 2024-25 is pre-April 2026
No — penalty appeals after April 2026 must use new Act provisions
Yes — but only if the taxpayer also files under Section 356 as backup

questions.explanation: AY 2024-25 is pre-April 2026, so the old Act governs all proceedings including penalty appeals. The appeal under old Section 246 filed within limitation is perfectly valid. No filing under the new Act is needed.
questions.explanationWrong: The old Act governs all proceedings for pre-April 2026 AYs — including penalty orders and their appeals. Old Section 246 is the correct provision, and filing within limitation makes it valid.

questions.question: A CA is handling a client's reassessment for AY 2022-23 and simultaneously preparing the return for Tax Year 2027-28. The reassessment show-cause is under old Section 148A. Which sections govern the reassessment notice if the AO proceeds?

options.text: New Section 280 for the notice
Old Section 148 for the notice
Old Section 148 but with new Section 282 time limits
New Section 281 for the show-cause and old Section 148 for the notice

questions.explanation: AY 2022-23 reassessment is entirely under the old Act. The show-cause is under old Section 148A, and the reassessment notice will be under old Section 148. New Act sections don't apply to pre-April 2026 AYs — there's no mixing of sections across Acts.
questions.explanationWrong: You can't mix sections from the two Acts. If the AY is pre-April 2026, the entire proceeding — show-cause (148A), notice (148), time limits (149), completion (153) — is under the old Act.

questions.question: For Tax Year 2028-29, the AO discovers escaped income of ₹75 lakh. What is the latest date by which the reassessment notice must be issued under the new Act?

options.text: June 30, 2035 (6 years 3 months from end of TY)
June 30, 2034 (5 years 3 months from end of TY)
March 31, 2035 (6 years from end of TY)
June 30, 2033 (4 years 3 months from end of TY)

questions.explanation: Tax Year 2028-29 ends March 31, 2029. ₹75 lakh exceeds ₹50 lakh, so the 6-year-3-month limit applies: March 31, 2029 + 6 years 3 months = June 30, 2035. The show-cause deadline would be March 31, 2035 (6 years).
questions.explanationWrong: ₹75 lakh is above the ₹50 lakh threshold. Under the new Act, the reassessment notice deadline is 6 years 3 months from end of TY: March 31, 2029 + 6y3m = June 30, 2035.

questions.question: In Unit 2.1, we learned about Section 536(2)(b). What does this sub-clause preserve after the repeal of the old Act?

options.text: Pending appeals only
Rights, privileges, obligations, and liabilities acquired under the old Act
Only tax credits and refunds
Only deductions claimed under the old Act

questions.explanation: Section 536(2)(b) is a broad savings clause that preserves all rights, privileges, obligations, and liabilities acquired, accrued, or incurred under the old Act. This is separate from pending proceedings (clause c) and is a catch-all for vested rights.
questions.explanationWrong: Section 536(2)(b) is a wide-ranging provision covering rights, privileges, obligations, and liabilities — not limited to any specific type. It's the vested-rights preservation clause.

questions.question: An assessee filed a revision application under old Section 264 (revision by Commissioner on application) on March 25, 2026. The Commissioner has not yet passed an order. After April 1, 2026, which Act governs the revision?

options.text: New Section 378 because it's the corresponding provision
Old Section 264 because the application was filed before April 1, 2026
New Section 378 but with old Act time limits
The Commissioner must ask the assessee to re-file under new Section 378

questions.explanation: The revision application under old Section 264 was pending on April 1, 2026. Section 536(2)(e) preserves all proceedings pending before any authority — the Commissioner will dispose of it under old Section 264, not new Section 378.
questions.explanationWrong: The application was filed and pending before April 1, 2026. Section 536(2)(e) explicitly preserves such proceedings — they continue as if the new Act had not been enacted.

questions.question: A practitioner needs to file an appeal for AY 2025-26 (old Act) and simultaneously challenge a reassessment for Tax Year 2026-27 (new Act). Which pair of sections does the practitioner use for filing these appeals with CIT(A)?

options.text: Old Section 246 for both appeals
New Section 356 for both appeals
Old Section 246 for AY 2025-26 and new Section 356 for TY 2026-27
New Section 356 for AY 2025-26 and old Section 246 for TY 2026-27

questions.explanation: This is the dual-track system in practice. AY 2025-26 is pre-April 2026 → old Section 246. Tax Year 2026-27 is the new Act → Section 356. The practitioner must know both Acts and apply each to the correct year.
questions.explanationWrong: Each year gets its own Act: old Section 246 for pre-April 2026 AYs, new Section 356 for Tax Year 2026-27 onwards. You cannot use one Act's provisions for the other year.
